words: SAM SCHRAGER: I'd say your old head is pretty good.
ARTHUR BJERKE: No, it ain't much good anymore. I can't, remember much.

speaker: SS
words: You know it seems to me you remember pretty good. You remember a lot more

speaker: SS
words: than most of the people that I speak to.

speaker: AB
words: Well, I was down on the ridge Monday and seen Ed Halseth. And I've known

speaker: AB
words: him since he was that high. He's over 70, he's pretty good yet. Anyway

speaker: AB
words: he can get around pretty good.

speaker: SS
words: You remember how we were talking about Bill Helmer and you surveying?

speaker: AB
words: Yeah?

speaker: SS
words: Well, I was thinking about that, and Iwas wondering about how you did it.

speaker: SS
words: I was wondering what kind of instruments you used to do the surveying.

speaker: AB
words: Well we generally used one of them tip-up, mostly a compass though. That

speaker: AB
words: is a compass with levels on it, so you raised them up, and then of course it

speaker: AB
words: was.so you had to set 'em level. Otherwise if you didn't set 'em level

speaker: AB
words: you'd be a-runnin« off. Well of course we might run off a little, but then

speaker: AB
words: of course we'd have to measure between the government stakes, and he'd keep

speaker: AB
words: track of so many chains that we measured, and if it didn't come back, then

speaker: AB
words: he'd know how many inches he'd have to set back every stake to get 'em in

speaker: AB
words: line.

speaker: SS
words: How did you decide where the corners were supposed to be?

speaker: AB
words: Well you know the government stakes was put down in the '80's up here. So

speaker: AB
words: that was quite awhile before our times, and of course the marks was all in

speaker: AB
words: the trees, that is on the corners. So we could find them pretty good. By

speaker: AB
words: gosh I'll bet I could go over in the Park country and find the marks that

speaker: AB
words: was put down in the '85's. And there is one just a half a mile down here,

speaker: AB
words: and the old mark that was put down in '85, and the old witness tree that's

speaker: AB
words: fell over, but it's still a-layin' there.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of mark did they put in the tree?

speaker: AB
words: Well they'd blaze it off, and then they had a kind of digger that they'd

speaker: AB
words: dig into the tree, so you could read 'em all right. And there is one stump

speaker: AB
words: right up here with the government mark in it yet.

speaker: SS
words: How did you decide if the government mark was right or your mark was right?

speaker: AB
words: Well we'd have to go by the government mark. We couldn't change that.

speaker: AB
words: There is no way of changing the government mark. Now over here at Camp 4

speaker: AB
words: at Potlatch, that is where there's two government corners in there. They're

speaker: AB
words: a couple hundred feet apart.

speaker: SS
words: How do you think that happened?

speaker: AB
words: Well, see one set of surveyors come from one side and another come from

speaker: AB
words: another side and that's where they come together. Well they established the

speaker: AB
words: two corners, so it's a corner of no man's land in there.

speaker: SS
words: Well what was the purpose of you andfelmer checking the old survey for?

speaker: AB
words: Well that was the Potlatch buying the timber, see, and they'd have to

speaker: AB
words: estimate the timber. Well they'd have to know where their lines was. Now

speaker: AB
words: that field over there is in four sections, two townships, range one and two.

speaker: AB
words: That's a government corner right over across the creek there. And I've kept

speaker: AB
words: that tils here three, four years ago, tils I wasn't able to farm myself, and

speaker: AB
words: by god they plowed it up. So the fella that was supposed to farm it wasn't

speaker: AB
words: here. He had a hired man and by god he plowed up the government corner.

speaker: SS
words: Was it a tree?

speaker: AB
words: No, it was a concrete block. And I don't know I never could find the block

speaker: AB
words: again.

speaker: SS
words: Did you and Helmer put new marks on the corners or did you just find the

speaker: SS
words: old corners?

speaker: AB
words: We just found the old corners and then, say the marks on the trees growed

speaker: AB
words: over. Well we'd have to chop off the wood so we could find the old mark in

speaker: AB
words: underneath...Old Bill Helmer was pretty good in the woods. It was no danger

speaker: AB
words: of that feller getting lost. And it wasn't much danger of me getting lost

speaker: AB
words: neither. I knew everything between Avon and Elk River pretty good, because

speaker: AB
words: I herded cattle along in 1903-4, for three years in through that country.

speaker: AB
words: Yes I knew the man that homesteaded where Elk River is.

speaker: SS
words: Who is that?

speaker: AB
words: Oh hell I can't think of his name. He was quite a fella to drink. He

speaker: AB
words: was a big man. Oh, I can't think of his name. (Old Man Trumball.)

speaker: SS
words: Was he the guy that had a lodge for hunters?

speaker: AB
words: Yah. Yeah that Bronson Meadow y'know, where the Shattucks and Hughes Meadow,

speaker: AB
words: used to be, they call it the Bronson now I think—that's where I had the camp.

speaker: AB
words: So it was just over the hill over to Elk River from there. And no fish in

speaker: AB
words: them creeks those days. There was no fish above Elk Creek Falls. And there

speaker: AB
words: was none in the Shattucks and Hughes Creeks, neither. It was some big falls

speaker: AB
words: on that creek.

speaker: SS
words: Did you see many people out there?

speaker: AB
words: Not very many. Nothing else than in the fall some fellas would come up

speaker: AB
words: and pheasant hunting more than anything else. And there wasn't hardly any

speaker: AB
words: deer in there then. And very seldom an elk'd be seen in that country.

speaker: AB
words: (Pause.)

speaker: SS
words: Why wasn't there game then?

speaker: AB
words: Well sir, you know there was too much snow up in there. The game didn't

speaker: AB
words: stay in there too much. They stayed down this way further, that is the deer.

speaker: AB
words: And the elk, they all was over on the Clearwater.

speaker: SS
words: Did this guy live out there on the Elk River himself? Did he have neighbors?

speaker: AB
words: Well he was mostly there by himself. Of course he had an old woman and a

speaker: AB
words: stepson there with him. And the stepson would always steal his whiskey.

speaker: SS
words: How did they get by out there?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, they'd get by all right. Of course they could down below the falls,

speaker: AB
words: that wasn't over three miles, and they could catch all the damn fish they

speaker: AB
words: wanted to down there. And that's mostly where a lot of them fellas come up,

speaker: AB
words: y'know, and stayed up there, and there was more of a gambling den than any

speaker: AB
words: thing else up there. And then of course there was quite a few Indians come

speaker: AB
words: in there, and in the summer it was lots of huckleberries back in there. The

speaker: AB
words: huckleberry pickers'd come in. And y'know, it didn't take much too much to

speaker: AB
words: live. Flour was pretty cheap, and one thing another.

speaker: SS
words: What did you do out there when you were herding the cattle? Did you have

speaker: SS
words: to stay with them on the range all the time?

speaker: AB
words: Well I'd go around them every day and keep 'om so that they didn't get too

speaker: AB
words: far spread out. I had about 400 head I was a-takin' care of. So it was

speaker: AB
words: just me and the dog and the horse. And the dog, I kept him at camp most of

speaker: AB
words: the time, because them damn bear y'know would come and want to get into camp.

speaker: AB
words: But the dog'd keep them out of camp all right.

speaker: SS
words: There was a lot of bear?

speaker: AB
words: Oh there was quite a few bear all right. But that's one thing I never shot,

speaker: AB
words: I never shot a bear, because I didn't want to eat them.

speaker: AB
words: (End of Side A)

speaker: SS
words: You say you didn't want to eat 'em?

speaker: AB
words: No, I didn't want to eat no bear.

speaker: SS
words: So you didn't want to kill 'em?

speaker: AB
words: No, what's the use of killing something you don't want to eat? The only

speaker: AB
words: thing I'd want to kill is coyotes, but boy I had it in for them. Because they

speaker: AB
words: were always a-killin' deer.

speaker: SS
words: Can you ever remember a time when there were wolves in this country?

speaker: AB
words: Wolves? Well there was one time there was three wolves come in to this

speaker: AB
words: country. I killed one of them, and I think old man Dillman and Gus (?)

speaker: AB
words: Anderson killed the other two. That's all the wolves I remember. And I

speaker: AB
words: never killed a cougar, I've seen 'em though. But I never killed a cougar.

speaker: AB
words: But coyotes I killed lot of them. And I killed quite a few dogs that was achasin'

speaker: AB
words: deer too. You know, a deer is a funny animal. If they are chased

speaker: AB
words: bad by coyotes or a dog, they might run right up to a person. I've had deer

speaker: AB
words: come so close to me that I could lay my hand m hn,that'd been chased. You

speaker: AB
words: wouldn't think that, would ya? But by gosh now, I had one deer that was

speaker: AB
words: bothered by a dog over there, by god he come right up to me and he followed

speaker: AB
words: me right up to the barn, after I beat hell out of the dog. No by gosh a deer

speaker: AB
words: y'know gets pretty tame if they are chased by dogs or coyote.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think he was coming to you for help, or was he just confused?

speaker: AB
words: I think he was a^comin' to me for help. Because when I knocked hell out

speaker: AB
words: of the dog and chased the dog out, by god the deer crawled through the fence

speaker: AB
words: and followed me right up to the barn. No, and a time or two there's been

speaker: AB
words: other deer y'know that have run up to fellers that way, that'd been chased

speaker: AB
words: by dogs or coyotes. Now up there at Helmet Ross, there was a deer that come

speaker: AB
words: in there, and she got tamer than the dickens. And she got so she'd come in

speaker: AB
words: the house and jump into the bed and sleep in bed. Oh a deer tames right down

speaker: AB
words: fast. They had a tame deer in Deary one year, that damn thing you know was

speaker: AB
words: just as tame as it could be. God, that'd follow you all over.

speaker: SS
words: Was the deer just loose in Deary?

speaker: AB
words: Yeah. Yeah, somebody had killed the mother of this fawn, and the game

speaker: AB
words: warden took into Deary and raised it there. And boy it got so damn tame,

speaker: AB
words: you know, it just wanted to come in the houses and everyplace else. No, you

speaker: AB
words: take a deer, you know, they go to a person for protection if they are bothered

speaker: AB
words: too much.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think a deer has to be a fawn to get really tame?

speaker: AB
words: No, I think they can get pretty tame if they're pretty grown. Well had one

speaker: AB
words: doe here, I think she'd been around some barns during the winter and been fed

speaker: AB
words: with cattle—she was so tame I could go as close to her as from here to the

speaker: AB
words: radio and stand and talk to her. But she wouldn't let me touch her. She

speaker: AB
words: was around here for two, three years, but I think finally somebody killed her.

speaker: AB
words: But she was really tame. I wouldn't have shot her on a bet. She was my best

speaker: AB
words: friend. No, they can get pretty friendly when they are around people.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think deer are pretty smart?

speaker: AB
words: Well I know they ain't dumb by any means. They are pretty smart all right.

speaker: AB
words: I know they can make a fool out of a person damned easy in the woods. A

speaker: AB
words: deer has made a fool of lots of hunters. (Pause.) I got a book from Ed Halseth

speaker: AB
words: yesterday, but you couldn't read that, it's Norwegian.

speaker: SS
words: You're right, I can't make out a word. I bet you can read it pretty good

speaker: SS
words: though.

speaker: AB
words: Oh I can read it all right.

speaker: SS
words: Could you read me a little bit of it, so I could hear how it sounds?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, I don't know. (Pause. He reads a few words.) Oh heck, if I could

speaker: AB
words: see it I could read it all right, but it's all blurred so bad.

speaker: SS
words: Could you speak a little to me then, not reading? Say something to me in

speaker: SS
words: Norwegian?

speaker: AB
words: (He recites.) That was Swede I spoke now. There's just one thing Iwish

speaker: AB
words: I'd done, that I'd went back to Denmark and then went around by Stockholm,

speaker: AB
words: Sweden, and back up through Norway. Because I could talk to all of them. I

speaker: AB
words: could get along pretty good with Denmark, and I could get along with the

speaker: AB
words: in Sweden. And the real Swedish language, I could get along pretty

speaker: AB
words: good with them because I could read that. And Norwegian, I could read that

speaker: AB
words: if I could see it.

speaker: SS
words: Is there much difference between Norwegians and Swedes?

speaker: AB
words: Oh there's quite a lot of difference in the pronounciation. But Denmark

speaker: AB
words: and Norway uses the book, the language. Denmark y'know and Norway

speaker: AB
words: used to be one country at one time.

speaker: SS
words: What about the people Arthur, the Swedes and the Norwegians? Were they very

speaker: SS
words: much alike? Did they have a lot in common when they came over here?

speaker: AB
words: Oh yeah, they get along pretty good. You take the Lutherm church down here

speaker: AB
words: that they started down below Deary, you know about half of the people that

speaker: AB
words: joined it was Swedes. There is lots of Swedes buried in that Lutheran

speaker: AB
words: cemetery down there. I think there is more Swedes than Norwegians buried

speaker: AB
words: down there.

speaker: SS
words: Well there were more Swedes than Norwegians that come out here, weren't

speaker: SS
words: there?

speaker: AB
words: Yeah, well from Deary down towards Troy it was more Swedes than Norwegians.

speaker: SS
words: Why did your family leave Norway and come out here?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, I don't know. Times pretty hard back there. And dad, y'know the place

speaker: AB
words: that dad had there, his father and his grandfather was all born on the place. And

speaker: AB
words: dad was born on the

speaker: AB
words: place and I was born in the house. So that's a pretty old house.

speaker: SS
words: Was there any difference, Arthur, in the customs between the Swedes and the

speaker: SS
words: Norwegians over here?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, not a great deal. They got along pretty good. And there wasn't too

speaker: AB
words: much difference. Yah, you know, that down in around in Troy they had all

speaker: AB
words: kinds of weather at one time down there. There was one fella by the name

speaker: AB
words: Winter, and one by the name Summer, and one by the name Storm. So they had

speaker: AB
words: winter, summer, and storm all at one time down there (laughs).

speaker: SS
words: Can you remember any of the customs that your family kept, that were

speaker: SS
words: Norwegian, when they came over here? Like holidays?

speaker: AB
words: Well now like Christmas, they'd do up all the work the day before Christmas.

speaker: AB
words: Well then they kept 13 holidays that they didn't do nothing else than visit

speaker: AB
words: neighbors and around like that. That was the old country customs and Easter,

speaker: AB
words: there was Easter Sunday and second day of Easter. The day after Easter was

speaker: AB
words: a holiday. And Good Friday was always a holiday. And like Christmas, there'd

speaker: AB
words: always be a Christmas tree and one thing another.

speaker: SS
words: When did you start to hunt, Arthur?

speaker: AB
words: Well, I started hunting when I was about 15 years old. I got a .22, that

speaker: AB
words: was my first gun. That'd be about 1903-4, I have an idea I started hunting

speaker: AB
words: somewheres in there.

speaker: SS
words: How did you learn?

speaker: AB
words: Well generally the first hunting I done was with a bow and arrow, and then

speaker: AB
words: I got a .22 and I done quite a little hunting with that. Then 1906, well

speaker: AB
words: before that, it must have been about 1900, I got the first big gun, that was

speaker: AB
words: a 44-40, but hell, you could run as fast as that bullet'd go. But then about

speaker: AB
words: 1906 I got a 30-30 and I've still got it. And the first shot I ever fired

speaker: AB
words: with that 30-30 I killed a deer. And the last shot I ever fired I killed a

speaker: AB
words: coyote with it.

speaker: SS
words: Was it deer that you hunted mostly?

speaker: AB
words: Yahl. Well there is one stump over across Corral Creek, that is on McKinney's

speaker: AB
words: place. There is one stump there that I used to stand on, and I'm the only

speaker: AB
words: man that ever killed a deer from that stump. And I've shot 26 deer off from

speaker: AB
words: that stump. I killed one big buck there that hogdressed 226 pounds.

speaker: AB
words: That was a big deer. We used to have some awful big deer in the early days,

speaker: AB
words: but none of them left. And you know the elk we had here, they were shipped

speaker: AB
words: in from the Yellowstone Park. They were shipped in in 1928. They were

speaker: AB
words: shipped to Bovill and kept up there at the company horse barn that winter.

speaker: AB
words: They had to beg for money to ship them elk in, so I put in eight, ten dollars

speaker: AB
words: to have them shipped in. George Lawrence put in ten dollars and Fritz Peter

speaker: AB
words: son put in ten dollars. It cost us about seven dollars a head to have 'em

speaker: AB
words: shipped in. We got thirty head.

speaker: SS
words: Did they take to the country good?

speaker: AB
words: Oh yeah, they took to the country right along. Hell they got so they fed

speaker: AB
words: clean down to Kendrick. Hell there was one time before they opened the sea

speaker: AB
words: son, I seen as high as 26 elk in that field over there, at one time. I seen

speaker: AB
words: 26 elk and 50 some odd deer one night. That's a lot of game. And now there

speaker: AB
words: is hardly anything left.

speaker: SS
words: What do you think caused that? All the game to disappear?

speaker: AB
words: Well, too damn much hunting going on, and too much damn much poisoning

speaker: AB
words: done. You take like the birds, y'know, all this farm business they had »em

speaker: AB
words: poison squirrels one year, a couple years. And they run around and poisoned

speaker: AB
words: around all the fields. Well they had poison grain, and they scattered that

speaker: AB
words: all around on top of the ground around the edges of the field. Well they

speaker: AB
words: poisoned the biggest part of the birds.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know which kinds of birds they poisoned?

speaker: AB
words: Well it was mostly grouse and pheasants, because they were a-workin' in the

speaker: AB
words: fields. When the grain'd start in getting ripe, they'd come in the fields

speaker: AB
words: and work on those grain heads that'd be lost and one thing another. Well

speaker: AB
words: they'd find that poison grain, well they'd eat that too, and of course that'd

speaker: AB
words: kill 'em.

speaker: SS
words: Did there used to be a lot of grouse and pheasant in .the old days?

speaker: AB
words: Well y'know, you could go out here, I can remember I could go out for ten

speaker: AB
words: minutes and have a dozen pheasant. And the way with grouse. God, there

speaker: AB
words: was grouse galore. There were three different breed of grouse. There was

speaker: AB
words: the hooters and the gray grouse and then the mountain grouse.

speaker: SS
words: What was the first kind you said, hoo doos?

speaker: AB
words: No, they'd sit and hoot in the trees in the morning. Well there was a lot

speaker: AB
words: of 'em'd just get up and go out in the morning and they'd hear »em hoot up

speaker: AB
words: in the trees and go out and kill 'em. And then we had the foolhens, and they

speaker: AB
words: were so damn foolish you could walk right up to 'em and kill 'em with a stick.

speaker: AB
words: (End of Side B)

speaker: SS
words: How come you could shoot so many deer from the stump on McKinney's

speaker: SS
words: place? Did the deer just used to gather there?

speaker: AB
words: They used to travel and I don't know, I was always lucky. It got so that

speaker: AB
words: when we would be a-huntin' in there, they wouldn't let nobody else go up to

speaker: AB
words: that stand but me, because they said that I was the only one that could kill

speaker: AB
words: a deer there. I never had one get away from me neither that I shot at up

speaker: AB
words: there. I got every one I shot at. Oh I was a little better shot than the

speaker: AB
words: biggest part of them.

speaker: SS
words: What size gun did you like to use on the deer?

speaker: AB
words: A 30-30, the old gun I've got now. That old gun must have killed

speaker: AB
words: close to a hundred deer and about 20 elks since I had it. Melvin Halseth

speaker: AB
words: was down the other night. He asked me if I remember when I stood out here

speaker: AB
words: and shot elk down there at the edge of the woods. Shooting over a quarter

speaker: AB
words: of a mile with a 30-30 and kill them three elk.

speaker: SS
words: You shot 'em one after another?

speaker: AB
words: Vup.

speaker: SS
words: What did you do with air the meat?

speaker: AB
words: Well, I had neighbors. Hell I never...I'd hunt with the neighbors, I was

speaker: AB
words: generally the lucky one that killed the game. One time I worked in Deary,

speaker: AB
words: and I worked tils quitting time, and I come home, and I haven't had time to

speaker: AB
words: hunt, it was the last of the hunting season. And I come home, and I stopped

speaker: AB
words: out here and I got out and I looked up on top of that hill there, and I

speaker: AB
words: thought I seen a cow in the field up there. I had fall wheat up there. I

speaker: AB
words: said, "Now whose damn cow is in the field up there." I stood and looked

speaker: AB
words: at her a little bit, it was getting pretty late in the evening, getting kind

speaker: AB
words: of dusk. But I could see her good agin' the sky light over there. And I

speaker: AB
words: looked and I said, "By god, that ain't a cow, that's an elk." So I got the

speaker: AB
words: gun out of the pickup, and I started in shooting. I shot nine times and I

speaker: AB
words: got two elk up there. And it was late in the evening, Melvin Peterson lived

speaker: AB
words: over here where Winn lives now, and he come out of the barn with a lantern.

speaker: AB
words: And I hollered at him, "Come on down here, I got some job to do." And Edwin

speaker: AB
words: Magnuson was over there, he come down, we went up and I had two elk up there.

speaker: AB
words: I took the tractor and the stone boat and went up and went up there, loaded

speaker: AB
words: them on, hauled »em down and hung »em up, skinned 'em. Well I give Melvin

speaker: AB
words: and Edwin—they both had family—the big elk, and then I kept the little one

speaker: AB
words: and I kept a half of that, and give the other half to another neighbor.

speaker: SS
words: Did your friends believe a smell gun like yours could do as well as a 30-06?

speaker: AB
words: No they used to laugh at me, but I'd generally kill »em just as far off as

speaker: AB
words: they did. I went up to the Selway one time, and there was three of us, and

speaker: AB
words: they made fun of my small gun. They had big guns, and we'd been in there

speaker: AB
words: quite awhile hunting, and we haven't got anything. And the last day we was

speaker: AB
words: in there, we's going down the trail and look around. And I says, "I wouldn't

speaker: AB
words: go down the trail with you fellas, I'll take the ridge fork, and you fellas

speaker: AB
words: can go down the trail." So I took the ridge and pretty soon I heard them

speaker: AB
words: starting in shooting to beat the devil down on the trail. Well I go down

speaker: AB
words: there and see what's a-goin' on. I got down there and by gosh they have shot

speaker: AB
words: all their cartridges, and there was a bull elk over across the canyon, a big

speaker: AB
words: canyon, it must have been about five or six hundred yards. And they said,

speaker: AB
words: "Oh shit, what are you going to do with that gun?"

speaker: AB
words: I says, "I can shoot just as well as you fellers can." Well they put

speaker: AB
words: out a new shell for the 30-30, 110 grain bullet. Well that put her up in the

speaker: AB
words: 2800. And I had some of them shells in the gun. I shot twice at the elk.

speaker: AB
words: And the first time I shot he kind of staggered, and I shot again and he walked

speaker: AB
words: ahead a couple steps and he went down. Well they said, "You sure as hell didn't

speaker: AB
words: hit him with that gun." And when we skinned him by god we found two bullets

speaker: AB
words: out of my gun in him and that was all he ever was hit. And I was shooting

speaker: AB
words: further than they did with the big gun. They didn't laugh so much at that

speaker: AB
words: little gun then. I killed that elk and a big buck over there, and a small

speaker: AB
words: deer, and that was all the game we got on that trip. I had the head on that

speaker: AB
words: big mule deer mounted but that burnt up when the house burnt up. I had three

speaker: AB
words: deer heads mounted that burnt up.

speaker: SS
words: The house here burnt up?

speaker: AB
words: Yeah. Yeah that burnt up. That part of this house is an old railroad car.

speaker: AB
words: I built on this part.

speaker: SS
words: How did it catch fire?

speaker: AB
words: Well I don't know. It started upstairs about four o'clock in the morning.

speaker: AB
words: And I haven't had fire in the stove since early the morning before, so it

speaker: AB
words: couldn't have been from the stove. But there was a lot of stuff cached up

speaker: AB
words: there. There was a woman that cooked here when they cut the timber in the

speaker: AB
words: Milwaukee down here. She had a lot of stuff cached up there, and I kind of

speaker: AB
words: think maybe it's been some matches or something in some of the clothes up

speaker: AB
words: there that some mice or something had gnawed on. So all her stuff burnt up.

speaker: AB
words: Oh I done quite a lot of carpenter work in the early days. Several houses

speaker: AB
words: in Helmer and some in Bovill that I've built, and some in Deary. And there

speaker: AB
words: is one south of Troy on the road between Troy and Kendrick that I've built.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember the old fires in Deary?

speaker: AB
words: Oh yeah I remember some of them. Yeah, I remember when it burnt pretty

speaker: AB
words: well in Deary and also in Bovill. (Pause.) Well there isn't too many of

speaker: AB
words: them oldtimers left in Bovill neither.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember what happened in either of those fires? How they started?

speaker: AB
words: Well, it's hard telling how they started. I think some of them in Deary

speaker: AB
words: was set. I always had that in--my mind but nobody could prove it.

speaker: SS
words: Why would they be set?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, just meanness. (Pause.)

speaker: SS
words: Did people get lost very much in the country? Oh when they were going

speaker: SS
words: hunting or going around, trying to get from one place to another?

speaker: AB
words: Oh not too much. 'Course there is one fella from Genesee that never have

speaker: AB
words: found that was lost back in back of Beal's Butte there. They never have

speaker: AB
words: found him. And then that banker from Palouse was lost in there. They think

speaker: AB
words: they found him but they ain't too sure about it.

speaker: SS
words: Did you like to go very far like in that country up in there, Beal's Butte,

speaker: SS
words: to get game, or did you like to stay around here?

speaker: AB
words: Oh I stayed mostly around here. I never hunted far away from home. About

speaker: AB
words: as far as I hunted from home was up here on Cherry Butte and back in on

speaker: AB
words: Vasser Meadows and Corral Creek.

speaker: SS
words: Did you like to go around and explore the country just for the heck of it?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, when I was with the cattle of course I looked over the country pretty

speaker: AB
words: good, so I was pretty well acquainted. Oh, I was up on Marble Creek and

speaker: AB
words: in that country some.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that the coyotes are pretty smart animals?

speaker: AB
words: You're goddamned right they are smart. You better not touch a trap that

speaker: AB
words: you are going to catch one of them in with your bare hands. If you do the

speaker: AB
words: coyote will never touch it. They're too smart for that. They got a pretty

speaker: AB
words: good scent. But once in awhile a coyote will crossbreed with a dog. And

speaker: AB
words: when you get one of them dogs you generally get a mean one. Well y'know

speaker: AB
words: that's how they used to raise the wolf hounds. They'd cross 'em with wolves

speaker: AB
words: to get sleigh dog, and I guess they were pretty vicious, big lot of 'em.

speaker: SS
words: How come you didn't want the coyotes to kill any deer?

speaker: AB
words: Well you know the snow would get deep and the deer'd break through the

speaker: AB
words: crust. There'd be alittle crust on the snow, and the coyotes'd run on top,

speaker: AB
words: and then they'd hamstring the deer and kill 'em and eat 'em. Hell, y'know,

speaker: AB
words: coyotes'd kill lots of deer. There used to be one place down here in the

speaker: AB
words: spring of the year where the deer'd winter. It was right on the breaks of

speaker: AB
words: the river, on the breaks of Potlatch. Iused to go down there every spring

speaker: AB
words: and I'd always kill a bobcat or two down in there, and coyotes that was akillin'

speaker: AB
words: deer. And there was afew lynx cats in there. They'd weigh about

speaker: AB
words: 35-40 pounds, they were pretty good size. The lynx cats, they had tassels

speaker: AB
words: on their ears, the bobcats didn't.

speaker: SS
words: Was there difference in their habits?

speaker: AB
words: Well you take them bobcats and the lynx cats, they'd jump on adeer's back.

speaker: AB
words: They'd jump out of a tree and onto their back, and then they'd cut their

speaker: AB
words: spinal cord. But the coyotes'd generally hamstring 'em. There is one place

speaker: AB
words: over across Corral Creek there, I shot only three deer on that place, but I

speaker: AB
words: killed three deer with two shot. Two was a-runnin' right abreast, right side

speaker: AB
words: to me, and I shot through one and killed the other one behind of it. And

speaker: AB
words: the third one I shot through two little pines and killed that behind of the

speaker: AB
words: two pine.

speaker: SS
words: Why do you think you're such a darn good shot?

speaker: AB
words: Well when Iherded cattle Ididn't have nothing else to do than to practice.

speaker: AB
words: And the boss, he had agun. He'd always come up every two weeks and bring

speaker: AB
words: some provision up to me, and we'd generally have ashooting match every night.

speaker: AB
words: And you know .22 cartridges, you could buy them by the box, ten cents a box

speaker: AB
words: for .22 shorts, or three boxes for 25 cents, so it didn't cost much to shoot.

speaker: AB
words: Well that's all I did shooting, there was thousands of chipmunks. Iwas

speaker: AB
words: shooting at chipmunks all the time. And then when Iwanted abird, I'd just

speaker: AB
words: take the gun out and kill the bird and have something to eat, have a bird

speaker: AB
words: for me and the dog.

speaker: SS
words: When you think back, do some times seem like they were good times, and some

speaker: SS
words: times like they were bad times?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, at time there were bad times all right. Iremember in -91 or 2, in

speaker: AB
words: the Cleveland's times, by god the times was tough. About the only thing

speaker: AB
words: we had was alittle bread and milk and eggs to eat. And Igot so tired of

speaker: AB
words: eggs that I never wanted to eat eggs since.

speaker: SS
words: What about the good times, which times were good times?

speaker: AB
words: Well, during the McKinley time we started in getting pretty good times

speaker: AB
words: again. And then during World's War Iit was pretty good times, and also in

speaker: AB
words: World's War II. The Potlatch camp was right over here during World's War II.

speaker: AB
words: There is one fella up at Bovill that was flunky in camp over here when the

speaker: AB
words: camp was over here. I seen him last summer. We had quite a visit.

speaker: SS
words: Uhat about the depression in 1929 and around in there? Was that areally

speaker: SS
words: hard time here?

speaker: AB
words: Oh yeah, it was pretty close. Yes, it was pretty hard time. Alot of the

speaker: AB
words: banks went hay...went flooey then. Yes, the bank in Deary went haywire, and

speaker: AB
words: the bank in Bovill.

speaker: SS
words: What about Troy?

speaker: AB
words: Well Troy was damned near gone, if it ain't been for old Green...
